title: Xia Legends of a Drift System

walkthrough.title: Shuffle the Sector Tiles
walkthrough.text: Let's get Xia set up. First, find the 21 Sector Tiles. These are the large hexagonal tiles that make up the game board. Shuffle them all face down into a single stack and place the stack within reach of all players. You will draw from this stack throughout the game as players explore new sectors of the drift system.

walkthrough.title: Deal Starting Sectors
walkthrough.text: Now deal out starting Sector Tiles face up into the center of the play area. Deal one tile per player: 3 tiles for a 3-player game, 4 for a 4-player game, 5 for a 5-player game. Place them one at a time, lining up the edge symbols on each tile with the previously placed tile. If you draw a Sector without a Spawn Point, shuffle it back into the deck and draw another. For your first game, the rulebook recommends using Burning Horse, Kemplar II, and Neo Damascus as starting sectors. Add Outpost 338 for 4 players and Expedior Gate for 5 players.

walkthrough.title: Place Exploration Tokens
walkthrough.text: Find the 21 Exploration Tokens. These are small round tokens that can reward you with 1 Fame Point, 1,000 Credits, or nothing. Shuffle them face down and place the pile within reach of all players. Now check the starting Sector Tiles for any Exploration Spaces. Place one face-down Exploration Token on each Exploration Space on the starting tiles. Do not peek at them.

walkthrough.title: Shuffle Mission and Title Decks
walkthrough.text: Find the 52 Mission Cards and the 18 Title Cards. These are two separate decks. Shuffle each deck face down and place them within reach of all players. Mission Cards are how you earn Credits and Fame by completing jobs around the system. Title Cards are special achievements that grant Fame Points and bonus Skills.

walkthrough.title: Set Out Shared Components
walkthrough.text: Place the following components in the center of the table where everyone can reach them: the 75 Cargo Cubes sorted by color (15 each of Spice orange, Holo pink, Terra green, Plasma teal, and Cyber purple), the 80 Damage Markers, the 84 Outfits organized by shape and type (Engines, Shields, Blasters, and Missiles in 3 tiers each, 7 of each type), the 40 Credit coins (30 silver 1,000-Credit coins and 10 blue 5,000-Credit coins), and the 4 dice: a d6, d8, d12, and d20.

walkthrough.title: Hand Out Starting Items
walkthrough.text: Give each player one How To Win Card for quick reference and 3,000 Credits worth of coins, which is 3 silver coins. These Credits are your starting funds to buy Outfits for your ship before the game begins.

walkthrough.title: Deal NPC Cards
walkthrough.text: Shuffle the 3 NPC Cards (Enforcer, Scoundrel, and Merchant) and deal one face up to each of the first three players. In a 4 or 5-player game, not everyone gets an NPC. Each player who receives an NPC Card takes the corresponding NPC Miniature and places it on the NPC Card. That player will control the NPC's actions throughout the game, but is not affiliated with the NPC in any way. For your very first game, the rulebook recommends ignoring NPCs entirely to keep things simpler.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Fame Point Track
walkthrough.text: Place the Fame Point Track in the play area where everyone can see it. Now your group decides how many Fame Points are needed to win. Place the Victory Marker on the track at that number. The rulebook suggests these playtimes: 5 Fame Points is about 15 minutes per player, 10 points is about 30 minutes per player, and 20 points is about 45 minutes per player. For your first game, play to 5 Fame Points to keep it short and sweet.

walkthrough.title: Choose Starting Ships
walkthrough.text: Separate the blue Tier 1 Ship Mats and their matching Ability Cards. Set aside the green Tier 2 and purple Tier 3 mats and cards for now. Each player rolls the d20. The player with the highest roll chooses a Tier 1 Ship Mat first and takes the matching Miniature and Ability Card. Selection continues counter-clockwise around the table until every player has a ship. Then all players place their Miniatures on the Spawn Point of the Sector Tile closest to them.

walkthrough.title: Set Up Your Ship Mat
walkthrough.text: Each player now takes an Impulse Token and a set of 6 colored Markers matching their color. Set up your Ship Mat as follows. Place the Impulse Token face up, yellow-side up, on the Impulse Space of your Ship Mat. Place one Marker on the Fame Point Track at 0, labeled Unknown. Place one Marker on the highest number of your Ship Mat's Energy Meter. Place the remaining four Markers on the Armed spaces of your Ship Mat. These Armed Markers are what you spend to use your Outfits during the game.

walkthrough.title: Purchase Starting Outfits
walkthrough.text: In the same order as ship selection, each player may now spend their 3,000 Credits to purchase Outfits. Outfits are placed into your Ship Mat's Hold. You can only buy what physically fits in the Hold. You are not required to spend all or any of your Credits at this point. Outfits come in four types: Engines for movement, Shields for defense, Blasters for close-range attack, and Missiles for long-range attack. For your first game, the rulebook suggests these loadouts: Explorer buys a Raptor-K Engine. Trader buys an M-22 Engine and Targe Shield. Worker buys a Halon Engine and Targe Shield. Pirate buys an M-22 Engine and Saker V Blaster.

walkthrough.title: Determine First Player
walkthrough.text: The player who picked their ship last takes the first turn. Play proceeds clockwise from that player. The game is now ready to start. Each turn consists of three phases: the Action Phase for flying, fighting, exploring, and missions; the Business Phase for recharging, repairing, and shopping at planets; and the Status Phase for refreshing abilities, arming markers, and claiming Fame.

walkthrough.title: Game Overview and Victory
walkthrough.text: Xia is a sandbox-style competitive space adventure for 3 to 5 players. Each player starts as the captain of a small Tier 1 starship. You fly around the system completing Missions, exploring new Sectors, trading Cargo Cubes, and battling other ships. The goal is to earn Fame Points. When your Marker on the Fame Point Track reaches the Victory Marker, the game is over and you win. There are nine ways to earn Fame Points: Exploration, Missions, Trade, Combat, Wealth, Ships, Kindness, Titles, and Luck.

walkthrough.title: Turn Structure: Three Phases
walkthrough.text: Each turn has three phases in order. First is the Action Phase, where you fly, fight, explore, trade, and work on Missions. You can take as many Actions as you want until you run out of resources or decide to stop. Second is the Business Phase, but only if you end your Action Phase on a planet. On a planet, you can recharge energy for free, repair damage, buy or sell Outfits, rearrange your Hold, buy a new ship, or buy Fame. Third is the Status Phase, where you claim any Fame earned this turn, draw Title Cards if you hit a Title space, refresh your Ability Card and Impulse Token, and arm your Markers by spending Energy. Once a phase has started, you cannot go back to a previous phase. Play proceeds clockwise.

walkthrough.title: Ships, Ship Mats, and Ability Cards
walkthrough.text: Your ship has three key components. The Ship Mat shows your ship's Hold, which is the grid where you store Outfits and Cargo Cubes. It also shows your Energy Meter, Armed and Disarmed spaces for your 4 Markers, the Impulse Space, and your Bounty area. The Miniature represents your ship on the board and occupies one hex space. No two ships can share the same space, but you can move through occupied spaces. Your Ability Card grants a unique power. To use it, spend the required Energy and follow the card's instructions, often rolling a d20. After use, the card is Exhausted and flipped face down until refreshed during the Status Phase. Ships come in three tiers: Tier 1 (blue), Tier 2 (green), and Tier 3 (purple). Higher tiers are bigger, more powerful, and more expensive.

walkthrough.title: Energy and Markers
walkthrough.text: Energy is your ship's rechargeable resource. You spend Energy to arm Markers, use Abilities, and scan new Sector Tiles. To spend Energy, move your Energy Marker down the meter. You recharge for free during the Business Phase on any planet. You have 4 Armed Markers. To use an Outfit, move one Armed Marker from the Armed space on your Ship Mat to a Use Space on the Outfit, then roll the corresponding die. During the Status Phase, you can re-arm spent Markers at a cost of 1 Energy each. If on a planet, arming Markers costs no Energy. If you have zero Energy and zero Armed Markers, you are Stranded and can only move using Impulse.

walkthrough.title: Outfits: Engines, Shields, Blasters, and Missiles
walkthrough.text: Outfits are the core of your Action Phase. There are four types, each in 3 tiers from cheap and small to expensive and powerful. Engines let you move. Spend an Armed Marker, roll the Engine's die, and move that many spaces. Blasters let you attack an adjacent ship at Range 1 with Line of Sight. Missiles let you attack a ship at Range 2 to 6 with Line of Sight. Shields are unique because they can be used outside your turn to defend against incoming attacks. Roll the Shield's die to absorb damage. Each Outfit has Use Spaces that limit how many times it can be activated per turn. Damage Markers placed on an Outfit reduce its die roll by 1 per Damage and block Use Spaces.

walkthrough.title: Movement and Impulse
walkthrough.text: There are two ways to move. First, use an Engine Outfit by spending an Armed Marker and rolling its die. You can move up to the number rolled, minus any Damage on that Engine. Unspent movement is discarded when the Move Action ends. Second, use Impulse once per turn for free. Flip your Impulse Token face down and move a number of spaces equal to your ship's Impulse Value. Impulse cannot be used for Ramming. During a Move Action, you can pause to take Minor Actions like Blind Jump, Collect Exploration Tokens, Collect Cargo Cubes, or Jettison Cargo, then continue moving. Non-Minor Actions like Scan Sector end your Move Action immediately.

walkthrough.title: Combat
walkthrough.text: Combat follows six steps. One, Acquire Target: check Range and Line of Sight. Blasters need Range 1, Missiles need Range 2-6. Line of Sight is blocked by Asteroids, Debris Fields, Stars, Planetary Shields, Planetary Entrances, and unexplored edges. Two, Declare Attack: name your target and which Outfits and Abilities you are using, then spend Armed Markers and Energy. Three, Declare Defense: the defender declares Shields and defensive Abilities, spending their resources. Four, Roll: attacker rolls first, then defender. Five, Resolve: subtract the Defense Value from the Attack Value. The remainder is Damage dealt. Six, Re-Arm: the defender may immediately re-arm Markers spent on Shields at 1 Energy per Marker. Ramming uses an Engine as an attack at Range 1, but the attacker also takes full damage from their own roll.

walkthrough.title: Damage and Death
walkthrough.text: When a ship takes damage, place Damage Markers into the Hold, one per space. The owning player chooses where to place them. Any Cargo Cube on a damaged space is immediately Jettisoned onto the board. Damage on an Outfit's Use Space disarms any Marker there and blocks that space. Each Damage Marker on an Outfit also reduces its die rolls by 1. When Damage Markers equal or exceed your Hold spaces, your ship is destroyed. On death: remove the Miniature from the board, discard any Active Mission, drop all Cargo Cubes in the space where you died (Cubes dropped in Stars are destroyed), and remove any Bounty. Your ship resets to full Energy, all Markers Armed, all Damage removed, Abilities refreshed. Tier 2 and Tier 3 ships skip their next turn. Tier 1 ships do not skip. On your next non-skipped turn, roll the d20 to Respawn on the Spawn Point closest to the number rolled.

walkthrough.title: Exploration
walkthrough.text: To explore, your ship must be on a space with an unexplored edge, and there must be tiles left in the deck. You have two options. Scan costs 1 Energy and is a full Action that ends any Move. Draw a Sector Tile, match its symbol to the edge you are exploring, and place it. Blind Jump is a Minor Action that costs 1 movement point. Declare which unexplored edge you want to enter, draw and place the tile, and you must move into the declared space, even if it is a Star that will destroy you. Since Blind Jump is Minor, you can continue your Move Action afterwards. When a new tile has an Exploration Space, place a face-down Exploration Token on it. The first player to land on an Exploration Space flips the token and claims the reward: 1 Fame Point, 1,000 Credits, or nothing.

walkthrough.title: Borders and Hazards
walkthrough.text: The game board has several border types that affect movement. Normal Space has no effect. Star Borders destroy any ship that crosses them. Asteroid Fields block Line of Sight; roll d20 to enter: 1-10 means take damage equal to the roll, 11-20 means safe passage. Shields can mitigate asteroid damage. Nebulae do not block Line of Sight; roll d20 to enter: 1-10 means lose Energy equal to the roll, 11-20 means no effect. Shields can reduce Nebula Energy loss. Debris Fields block Line of Sight; roll d20 to enter: 1-3 means instant death, 4-20 means safe passage. Planetary Shields block Line of Sight; crossing them requires a d20 roll: 1-10 means the shield blocks you and you take damage equal to the roll, 11-17 means you pass but gain 1,000 Credits of Bounty, 18-20 means clean passage. Planet Entrances are the legal way in and out but have rules based on planet type. Gates let you spend 1 movement to teleport to any other Gate space.

walkthrough.title: Missions
walkthrough.text: On a Mission Point, if you have no Active Mission, take the Draw Missions Minor Action: draw 3 Mission Cards, keep at most 1, discard the rest. Your chosen Mission is placed face down as your Active Mission. You can only have 1 Active Mission at a time. There are six Mission types in two categories. Lawful Missions: Shuttle (pick up passengers, deliver to another planet), Research (conduct research at a Mission Point with a d20 roll, deliver results), and Bounty Hunter (destroy an Outlaw ship, collect remains with a d20 roll, deliver remains). Outlaw Missions: Smuggler (pick up contraband, sneak past planetary detection with a d20 roll to deliver), Thief (fly adjacent to target ship, steal with a d20 roll, deliver goods), and Assassin (destroy a specific player's ship, deliver remains). Once objectives are complete, reveal the Mission Card, place Payment on it, then fly to the Delivery Mission Point and take the Complete a Mission Action. Each completed Mission earns 1 Fame Point plus Credits.

walkthrough.title: Cargo Cubes and Trading
walkthrough.text: There are 5 types of Cargo Cubes: Spice (orange), Holo (pink), Terra (green), Plasma (teal), and Cyber (purple). You can acquire Cargo Cubes three ways. Buy: on a planet's Buy Space, pay 1,000 Credits for 2 Cubes. Mine Asteroid Fields, Salvage Debris Fields, or Harvest Nebulae by taking the corresponding Action and rolling d20: success on 11-20 gives 1 Cube. Mining failure (1-10) deals damage, Salvage failure (1-3) is instant death, Harvest failure (1-10) drains Energy. You can also Collect jettisoned Cubes from the board as a Minor Action. To sell, use a planet's Sell Space and sell matching Cubes for 1,000 Credits each. If you sell all the Cargo Cubes in your Hold and you sold at least 2, you earn 1 Fame Point for Trade.

walkthrough.title: Bounty and Ship Status
walkthrough.text: Ships are either Innocent (no Bounty) or Outlaw (has Bounty). You gain Bounty by: crossing a Planetary Shield and rolling 11-17 (1,000 Credits added to Bounty), completing or failing certain Outlaw Missions, or destroying an Innocent ship (1,000 Credits added). Outlaw ships cannot enter Lawful planet Entrances. Innocent ships entering an Outlaw planet Entrance gain 1,000 Credits of Bounty. When an Outlaw ship is destroyed, the attacker claims all the Bounty Credits. Bounty is removed on death or when purchasing a new ship.

walkthrough.title: Upgrading Your Ship and Buying Fame
walkthrough.text: During the Business Phase on a planet, you can purchase a ship exactly one Tier higher than your current ship. Tier 2 ships cost 5,000 Credits, Tier 3 ships cost 8,000 Credits. When you upgrade, your old Ship Mat and Miniature are replaced, but you keep all previous Ability Cards, Outfits, Cargo Cubes, and Credits. Damage and Bounty are not transferred. Buying a new ship earns you 1 Fame Point. You can also buy Fame directly: spend 5,000 Credits for 1 Fame Point during Business Phase, but you cannot purchase the final Fame Point needed for victory.

walkthrough.title: Titles and the Nine Ways to Win
walkthrough.text: Title Cards are drawn when any player first reaches a purple Title space on the Fame Point Track. The Title is read aloud and placed face up for all to see. Any player can compete to claim it by fulfilling its objective. Once claimed during your Status Phase, you receive the Fame Points (1 to 3) and a Skill, which is a small permanent ability. Remember the nine paths to Fame: Exploration Tokens worth 1 Fame. Completing Missions worth 1 Fame each. Trading earns 1 Fame for selling all your Cargo. Destroying ships earns Fame equal to the destroyed ship's tier. Wealth lets you buy 1 Fame for 5,000 Credits. Buying a new ship is 1 Fame. Rescuing a Stranded player is 1 Fame for Kindness. Claiming Titles is 1 to 3 Fame. Rolling a natural 20 on any d20 roll earns 1 Fame for Luck, maximum once per turn.

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Drift System
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted opening with three players. Player 1 will be our Explorer flying a ship with the Raptor-K Engine. Player 2 will be our Trader with an M-22 Engine and Targe Shield. Player 3 will be our Pirate with an M-22 Engine and Saker V Blaster. We are using the recommended first-game setup: Burning Horse, Kemplar II, and Neo Damascus as starting Sector Tiles, playing to 5 Fame Points with no NPCs. Follow along even if your loadouts differ. Ready? Let's fly.

walkthrough.title: Starting Positions and Resources
walkthrough.text: Each player has placed their Miniature on the Spawn Point of the Sector Tile closest to them. Every ship starts with full Energy, all 4 Markers Armed, and the Impulse Token face up. Player 1 (Explorer) has 1,000 Credits left after buying the Raptor-K Engine. Player 2 (Trader) has 0 Credits after buying the M-22 Engine and Targe Shield. Player 3 (Pirate) has 0 Credits after buying the M-22 Engine and Saker V Blaster. Remember: each turn has three phases: Action, Business (planet only), and Status.

walkthrough.title: Player 1's Turn: Exploring the Unknown
walkthrough.text: Player 1 has the Raptor-K Engine, which is excellent for exploration. Player 1 declares a Move Action using the Raptor-K Engine, moves an Armed Marker to the Engine's Use Space, and rolls the corresponding die. Let's say the roll is a 5. Player 1 moves toward the edge of the map, toward an unexplored edge. After spending 3 movement to get adjacent to an unexplored edge, Player 1 takes a Blind Jump Minor Action, spending 1 of the remaining movement points. A new Sector Tile is drawn, the symbol is matched, and the tile is placed. Player 1 moves into the new Sector. There is an Exploration Space on the new tile, so a face-down Exploration Token is placed on it. Player 1 still has 1 movement left, so they move onto the Exploration Space and collect the token. It's a Fame Point. Player 1 earns 1 Fame. Nice. Since Player 1 is not on a planet, the Business Phase is skipped. In the Status Phase, Player 1 claims the Fame Point, moving their Marker to 1 on the Fame Track. They refresh their Impulse Token and Ability Card, then spend 1 Energy to re-arm the spent Marker.

walkthrough.title: Why Exploration Was Smart
walkthrough.text: Player 1 earned a Fame Point on their very first turn through a combination of movement and the Blind Jump Minor Action. Exploring is one of the fastest ways to earn Fame early in the game because Exploration Tokens can yield immediate Fame Points. The key is that Blind Jump is a Minor Action, meaning it does not end your Move. You can keep moving after the jump to land exactly where you want. The Raptor-K Engine is ideal for this strategy because it gives you consistent movement range.

walkthrough.title: Player 2's Turn: Setting Up a Trade Route
walkthrough.text: Player 2 is on Kemplar II, which has a Buy Space for Spice Cargo Cubes. But Player 2 has 0 Credits after buying Outfits. So Player 2 needs to earn Credits first. Player 2 declares a Move Action using Impulse. They flip the Impulse Token face down and move toward the nearest Mission Point, which is on Kemplar II. On the Mission Point, Player 2 takes the Draw Missions Minor Action: draw the top 3 Mission Cards, look at them, and choose one Shuttle Mission that delivers passengers to Neo Damascus, which is right next door. Player 2 places the Mission face down as their Active Mission. Player 2 then takes the Pick Up Passengers Action at the Mission Point. The Mission is revealed: the payment is based on the shortest flyable distance between the pick-up and delivery Sectors, which is 1 Sector. Payment Credits are placed on the card. Now Player 2 needs to fly to Neo Damascus to deliver. In the Status Phase, Player 2 refreshes Impulse and spends 1 Energy to re-arm any spent Markers.

walkthrough.title: Player 3's Turn: The Pirate's Mistake — Rushing to Attack
walkthrough.text: Player 3 sees Player 2's ship and gets excited. They have a Saker V Blaster and want to fight. Player 3 declares a Move Action using their M-22 Engine. They spend an Armed Marker, roll the die, and move adjacent to Player 2. Then Player 3 declares an Attack Action with the Blaster, spending another Armed Marker. But here's the problem. Player 3 rolled a modest attack, let's say a 3. Player 2 declares defense with the Targe Shield, spending an Armed Marker. Player 2 rolls the Shield and gets a 5. The Defense Value of 5 exceeds the Attack Value of 3, so zero damage is dealt. Player 3 just spent 2 Armed Markers and accomplished nothing. They are now down to 2 Armed Markers and have no Credits, no Mission, and no plan for earning Fame. In the Status Phase, Player 3 re-arms by spending 2 Energy. That is a lot of Energy burned for zero gain.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Don't Attack Without a Plan
walkthrough.text: Player 3's mistake is a classic beginner trap. Attacking feels exciting but costs Armed Markers and Energy, your most precious resources early in the game. Unless you can deal enough damage to actually destroy a ship and earn the Combat Fame Point, you are just draining your own resources. In a Tier 1 ship, your Blaster die is small, and a defender with even a basic Shield can often block your entire attack. The lesson: early-game aggression without overwhelming firepower is a waste. Focus on building up first, then fight when you can finish the job.

walkthrough.title: Player 1's Second Turn: Pressing the Exploration Advantage
walkthrough.text: Player 1 is back. With 1 Fame already banked, they want more. Player 1 uses an Engine Move Action to fly toward another unexplored edge. This time they use a Scan Action instead of Blind Jump. Scanning costs 1 Energy but is safer because you see the tile before you enter it. The new Sector has an Exploration Space. Player 1 still has movement from the Engine roll, so they use Impulse (their second Move Action this turn) to reach the Exploration Space. The token reveals 1,000 Credits! Not Fame, but still valuable. Player 1 now has 2,000 Credits total. In the Status Phase, Player 1 claims their rewards and re-arms.

walkthrough.title: Player 2's Second Turn: Completing the Mission
walkthrough.text: Player 2 declared a Move Action with the M-22 Engine. They rolled well and fly through the Planetary Entrance into Neo Damascus. The Entrance is neutral, so no special rules apply. Player 2 moves onto the Mission Point and takes the Complete a Mission Action. The passengers are delivered. Player 2 earns 1 Fame Point for completing the Mission plus the Credits that were placed on the Mission Card. Now Player 2 is on a planet, so the Business Phase activates. They recharge Energy for free, and with their newly earned Credits, Player 2 buys 2 Spice Cargo Cubes from the Buy Space for 1,000 Credits. These will be sold later for profit and potentially another Fame Point. In the Status Phase, Player 2 moves their Fame Marker to 1 and re-arms for free since they are on a planet.

walkthrough.title: Player 3's Mistake: Forgetting the Business Phase
walkthrough.text: Player 3 is frustrated from the failed attack. They decide to fly to a planet to regroup. Player 3 uses their Engine to move toward Neo Damascus. But here is the mistake: Player 3 rolls the Engine, moves 4 spaces, and flies right past the Planetary Entrance and through the Planetary Shield instead. They have to roll the d20. The roll is a 14. That means Player 3 gets through, but they now have 1,000 Credits of Bounty on their Ship Mat. Player 3 is now an Outlaw. On top of that, Player 3 does not realize that as an Outlaw, they cannot use the Entrance of any Lawful planet to get back out. They will have to sneak through the Shield again to leave.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Use the Entrance, Not the Shield
walkthrough.text: Always use the Planetary Entrance to enter a planet legally. Crossing a Planetary Shield is risky for three reasons. On a roll of 1-10, you bounce off and take damage equal to the roll. On 11-17, you get through but gain Bounty. Only 18-20 is clean passage. Becoming an Outlaw limits where you can go and paints a target on your back. The only reason to cross a Shield is if you are already an Outlaw and cannot use the Entrance, or if you are running a deliberate Outlaw strategy. Player 3 should have simply entered through the Entrance with no risk.

walkthrough.title: Player 3's Mistake: Ignoring Missions Entirely
walkthrough.text: Player 3 is now on Neo Damascus with Bounty. During the Business Phase, they recharge Energy and repair any damage. But Player 3 still has no Active Mission and no Cargo Cubes. They spent their Credits on Outfits during setup and have earned nothing since. Meanwhile, Player 1 has 1 Fame from Exploration and 2,000 Credits. Player 2 has 1 Fame from a completed Mission, Credits, and Cargo Cubes ready to sell. Player 3 has 0 Fame and is an Outlaw with a Bounty. The lesson is clear: even Pirates need a plan for earning Fame. Missions, Trade, and Exploration all generate both Credits and Fame. Pure combat without income leaves you falling behind.

walkthrough.title: Player 3's Recovery: Drawing a Mission
walkthrough.text: On their next turn, Player 3 decides to get smart. They are on a planet with a Mission Point. Since they have no Active Mission, Player 3 takes the Draw Missions Minor Action. They draw 3 Mission Cards and find an Assassin Mission targeting a specific player. This is an Outlaw Mission, which fits Player 3's Pirate style. They accept it and place it face down. Now Player 3 has a goal. They also spend some time at the Business Phase: they can buy Cargo Cubes if they have Credits, or just plan to earn from the Mission. Player 3 is back in the game with a purpose.

walkthrough.title: The Trade Fame Point in Action
walkthrough.text: Meanwhile, Player 2 has been flying with Spice Cargo Cubes. They arrive at Neo Damascus's Sell Space. Player 2 takes a Sell Cargo Cubes Action and sells both Spice Cubes for 1,000 Credits each, earning 2,000 Credits total. Because Player 2 sold all the Cargo Cubes in their Hold and sold at least 2, they also earn 1 Fame Point for Trade. Player 2 is now at 2 Fame Points. This is the power of the Trading strategy: reliable Credits plus Fame in a single action.

walkthrough.title: Continue Playing
walkthrough.text: Play continues clockwise. Each player keeps taking turns with the three phases: Action, Business, Status. As you explore more Sectors, the board grows and new opportunities appear. More Mission Points, more Buy and Sell Spaces, more hazards, and more places to hide. Keep an eye on the Fame Point Track. When someone approaches the Victory Marker, the tension rises. Alliances may form and break. Outlaws will be hunted. Traders will be robbed. Explorers will push into the unknown. That is Xia.

walkthrough.title: Status Phase Reminder
walkthrough.text: At the end of every turn, do not forget the Status Phase. The four steps in order are: 1. Claim Fame by moving your Marker on the Fame Track. 2. Draw Title Cards if you are the first player to reach a purple Title space. 3. Refresh your Ability Card and Impulse Token by flipping them face up. 4. Arm Markers by spending 1 Energy each, or free if on a planet. Skipping the Status Phase means you start your next turn with fewer Armed Markers and an Exhausted Ability Card. That can be devastating.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations, Captain
walkthrough.text: You have seen all the major systems in action: Exploration with Blind Jump and Scan, Trading with Buy and Sell Cargo Cubes, Missions with Draw and Complete, Combat with Attack and Defense, and the pitfalls of becoming an accidental Outlaw. You watched Player 3 make three common mistakes: attacking without enough firepower, illegally crossing a Planetary Shield, and ignoring Missions. You saw how Player 1 and Player 2 earned Fame efficiently through Exploration and Trading. You are ready to chart your own course through the Drift System.

walkthrough.title: Before You Launch
walkthrough.text:
Before you start a full game, here are three things that can help.

First — check out General Tips for strategy basics on when to explore, trade, fight, or upgrade.

Second — reference the Appendix for a quick guide to every ship, Outfit type, Cargo Cube, Mission type, and border hazard.

And most importantly — ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Fly safe, Captain.

summary.title: Before You Launch
bullets: Check out General Tips for strategy basics
Reference the Appendix for ships, Outfits, Cargo, Missions, and borders
Ask your personal GM Guide — tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before diving into strategy, here are the most common mistakes new players make. First, attacking early with a Tier 1 Blaster. The dice are too small and defenders with even a basic Shield will block most of your damage. You waste Armed Markers and Energy for nothing. Second, crossing Planetary Shields when the Entrance is available. You risk damage and Bounty for no reason. Third, ignoring Missions entirely. Missions are one of the most reliable sources of both Credits and Fame. Fourth, forgetting the Status Phase steps. If you do not re-arm your Markers, you start the next turn crippled. Fifth, hoarding Cargo Cubes without selling them. Cubes in your Hold score nothing unless sold. Sell all your Cargo for the Trade Fame Point.

walkthrough.title: Choose a Strategy Early
walkthrough.text: Xia rewards players who commit to a strategy rather than trying to do everything at once. The four classic archetypes from the rulebook are Explorer, Trader, Worker, and Pirate. The Explorer buys a fast Engine and races to uncover new Sectors, collecting Exploration Tokens for instant Fame and Credits. The Trader buys an Engine and Shield, purchases Cargo Cubes, and sells them at matching planets for Credits and the Trade Fame Point. The Worker takes Shuttle and Research Missions for steady income. The Pirate arms up with a Blaster or Missile and hunts for Combat Fame and Bounties. Pick the approach that fits your starting ship's Ability Card and the Sector layout, then lean into it.

walkthrough.title: Manage Your Armed Markers Carefully
walkthrough.text: You only have 4 Armed Markers and each one costs 1 Energy to re-arm. Every Outfit activation costs a Marker, so plan your turn before spending. If you use 2 Markers on Engines and 1 on a Blaster, you only have 1 left for defense or a second move. Ending your turn on a planet is ideal because you recharge Energy for free and can arm Markers for free during the Status Phase. If you are not on a planet, each re-arm costs precious Energy. Running out of both Energy and Markers means being Stranded, which is a devastating setback.

walkthrough.title: Use Impulse Wisely
walkthrough.text: Impulse gives you free movement once per turn without spending Energy or Armed Markers. This is incredibly valuable. Use Impulse for that last bit of movement to reach a planet, a Mission Point, or a Sell Space. Combine Impulse with an Engine Move Action to maximize your range in a single turn. Remember that Impulse cannot be used for Ramming, and you can only use it once per turn. The Impulse Token refreshes during the Status Phase, so you get it back every turn.

walkthrough.title: End Your Turn on a Planet When Possible
walkthrough.text: Landing on a planet at the end of your Action Phase unlocks the Business Phase, which offers huge advantages. You recharge your Energy Meter to full for free. You can repair all Damage for just 1,000 Credits (Tier 1 and 2) or 2,000 Credits (Tier 3). You can buy and sell Outfits, rearrange your Hold, buy Cargo Cubes, sell Cargo Cubes, and even buy a new ship. Most importantly, arming Markers during the Status Phase on a planet is free. A turn that ends in deep space with spent Markers and low Energy is a wasted opportunity.

walkthrough.title: Understand the Fame Economy
walkthrough.text: There are nine ways to earn Fame, but some are more efficient than others depending on the game state. Early game, Exploration is fastest because you can earn Fame from tokens on your very first turn. Missions provide 1 Fame each and are repeatable. Trading requires at least 2 Cargo Cubes sold from a full Hold but gives both Credits and Fame. Combat is high-risk-high-reward: destroying a Tier 1 ship is 1 Fame, Tier 2 is 2 Fame, Tier 3 is 3 Fame. Buying a ship gives 1 Fame but requires 5,000 or 8,000 Credits. Buying Fame directly costs 5,000 Credits per point but cannot win you the game since you cannot purchase the final Fame Point. Luck from rolling natural 20s is a bonus, not a strategy. Titles range from 1 to 3 Fame and are competed for by all players.

walkthrough.title: When to Upgrade Your Ship
walkthrough.text: Upgrading from Tier 1 to Tier 2 costs 5,000 Credits and earns 1 Fame Point. The new ship has a bigger Hold, more Energy, and usually a better Impulse Value. You also gain a second Ability Card, keeping all previous ones. However, Tier 2 and Tier 3 ships skip their next turn when destroyed, while Tier 1 ships do not. This means upgrading increases your vulnerability to death. Upgrade when you have built up a solid Credit reserve, have a clear path to use the bigger Hold, and are confident you will not be destroyed immediately after. The Tier 2 ship at 5,000 Credits plus 1 Fame is often worth it mid-game. The Tier 3 ship at 8,000 Credits is a late-game power play.

walkthrough.title: The Outlaw Decision
walkthrough.text: Going Outlaw is a deliberate strategic choice, not an accident. Outlaw Missions (Smuggler, Thief, Assassin) tend to pay more Credits than Lawful ones, and destroying other ships for Combat Fame can be lucrative. However, Outlaws cannot enter Lawful planets through the Entrance, which severely limits your access to Buy and Sell Spaces and Business Phase benefits. Other players can destroy your ship and claim your Bounty Credits. If you go Outlaw, commit to it. Plan your routes around Outlaw and Neutral planets. Use the Smugglers' Den for trading. And remember that Bounty is removed when you die or buy a new ship, so you can reset to Innocent status if needed.

walkthrough.title: The Explorer Engine
walkthrough.text: The Explorer strategy is one of the fastest paths to early Fame. Buy the best Engine you can afford, ideally the Raptor-K for its consistent movement die. Your goal is to reach unexplored edges as quickly as possible and use Blind Jump to discover new Sectors. Blind Jump is a Minor Action, so you can continue moving after the jump to land on Exploration Spaces immediately. Exploration Tokens give you either 1 Fame Point, 1,000 Credits, or nothing. Even the duds help you by revealing the board for future turns. The key insight is that Exploration is non-competitive. No one else can take your token once you reach it. In a game to 5 Fame Points, if you can find 2-3 Fame tokens in the first few turns, you are already halfway to victory while other players are still setting up trade routes.

walkthrough.title: Advanced Exploration: Scan vs. Blind Jump
walkthrough.text: Scan costs 1 Energy and is a full Action that ends your Move, but it lets you see the tile before entering. Blind Jump costs 1 movement point and is a Minor Action, so you can keep moving, but you must enter the declared space regardless of what appears. The advanced play is to Blind Jump when you have plenty of movement left and Health to spare, and Scan when you are low on resources and cannot afford to stumble into a Star or Debris Field. Also consider that Scanning reveals the Sector to all players, potentially letting opponents plan their own routes. Blind Jumping gets you onto the new tile immediately, often claiming the Exploration Token before anyone else can react.

walkthrough.title: The Trader's Profit Loop
walkthrough.text: Trading is the most consistent Fame engine in Xia. The loop is simple: buy 2 or more Cargo Cubes at a Buy Space for 1,000 Credits per 2 Cubes, fly to a matching Sell Space, sell all Cubes for 1,000 Credits each, and earn 1 Fame Point if you empty your Hold with at least 2 Cubes sold. The profit margin is always positive because you spend 1,000 Credits for 2 Cubes and sell each for 1,000 Credits, doubling your money. A Tier 1 ship with 4-5 Hold spaces can carry 2-3 Cargo Cubes after Outfits. Upgrade to Tier 2 for a bigger Hold and carry 4-6 Cubes per trip, dramatically increasing Credits per run. The most profitable routes connect Kemplar II (buy Spice) to Neo Damascus (sell Spice) because they are often adjacent.

walkthrough.title: Advanced Trading: Mining, Salvaging, and Harvesting
walkthrough.text: You do not have to buy Cargo Cubes at planets. Mining Asteroid Fields, Salvaging Debris Fields, and Harvesting Nebulae are free actions that yield 1 Cube per successful d20 roll. Mining success is 11-20, but failure (1-10) deals damage equal to the roll. Salvage success is 4-20, but failure (1-3) is instant death. Harvest success is 11-20, but failure (1-10) drains Energy. You can attempt these actions multiple times per turn on the same space. With a Shield Outfit, you can mitigate Mining damage and Harvest Energy loss. The advanced play is to park on a Mining space near a Sell planet, mine until your Hold is full, then sell everything in one trip for a massive payout. This avoids the 1,000 Credit buy cost entirely, maximizing pure profit.

walkthrough.title: The Combat Specialist
walkthrough.text: Combat earns Fame equal to the destroyed ship's Tier: 1 for Tier 1, 2 for Tier 2, 3 for Tier 3. You also collect any Bounty on the destroyed ship. The problem is that Tier 1 weapons are weak and defenders can block with Shields. To be an effective combat player, you need to combine multiple attacks in a single Action. Spend multiple Armed Markers on the same weapon or combine a Blaster and a Missile in one Attack. The dice add together for your Attack Value, which is harder for a defender to fully block. Upgrade to a ship with stronger dice early. Target ships with Bounties for the Credit bonus. Target ships carrying Cargo Cubes because the Cubes drop when the ship is destroyed, and you can Collect them.

walkthrough.title: Advanced Combat: Ramming and Abilities
walkthrough.text: Ramming uses an Engine Outfit as a Range 1 attack. You roll the Engine die for damage, but you take the full damage yourself, and that self-damage is unblockable. Ramming is a desperation move, not a primary tactic. It is best used when your ship is already heavily damaged and you want to take an enemy down with you. Some Ability Cards provide powerful offensive or defensive options. Read your Ability Card carefully. Some Abilities add damage to attacks, provide defensive bonuses, or grant special movement. Since Ability Cards are unique to each ship, your ship's Ability should inform your combat style. Upgrading to Tier 2 gives you a second Ability Card, and Tier 3 gives a third, creating powerful combinations.

walkthrough.title: The Mission Grinder
walkthrough.text: Missions are repeatable Fame. Each completed Mission earns 1 Fame Point plus Credits. The key to grinding Missions efficiently is the Experience mechanic: a previously completed Mission of the same type can be discarded to re-roll a failed d20 on a new Mission of that type. This means your first Research Mission might fail, but your second and third become much more reliable because you can re-roll bad results. Stack completed Missions of one type to build up re-roll insurance. Shuttle Missions are the safest because they require no d20 roll for their objective, just flying from point A to point B. Research Missions offer variable payouts based on the d20 roll quality. Bounty Hunter Missions combine combat with delivery for double Fame potential.

walkthrough.title: The Outlaw Path
walkthrough.text: Outlaw Missions pay more Credits than Lawful ones. Smuggler Missions reward based on flyable distance and can be very lucrative for long routes. Thief Missions let you steal from other players without destroying their ships. Assassin Missions target a specific player and pay based on their ship's Tier. The risk is the Bounty that accumulates, making you a target and restricting planet access. The advanced Outlaw strategy uses the Smugglers' Den as a home base since it sells all Cargo types and has no entry restrictions. Route planning becomes critical because you must avoid or sneak through Lawful planet Shields. Pair this with combat readiness so you can defend yourself when hunted. Remember: buying a new ship removes your Bounty, so a well-timed upgrade can reset your status.

walkthrough.title: Title Racing
walkthrough.text: Titles appear when the first player reaches a purple space on the Fame Point Track. Once drawn, any player can compete to claim it. Titles are worth 1 to 3 Fame Points and grant permanent Skills. The advanced play is to track what Title spaces are coming up and position yourself to complete likely objectives before the Title is even drawn. If you are close to the lead, you will likely be the one drawing the Title, giving you first knowledge of its objective. Some Titles reward specific actions like exploring a certain number of Sectors or destroying a ship of a specific tier. Others reward accumulating Credits or completing multiple Missions. Skills from claimed Titles compound over the game, giving you small but meaningful advantages.

walkthrough.title: Endgame and the Final Push
walkthrough.text: As you approach the Victory Marker, every other player becomes your opponent. Someone at 4 Fame in a game to 5 is one Exploration Token, one Mission, or one ship kill away from winning. The advanced endgame involves both accelerating your own Fame gain and slowing the leader. Attack the leader's ship to destroy their Cargo Cubes and waste their turn through the death-respawn cycle (especially effective against Tier 2 and 3 ships that skip a turn). Block key Sell Spaces by occupying them. Draw Missions from the same Mission Points to deplete the deck near the leader. Conversely, if you are the leader, avoid combat, plan safe routes, and have multiple Fame-earning options ready so you can pivot if one path is blocked. Remember that you cannot buy the final Fame Point, so you need a concrete plan to earn it through Exploration, Missions, Trade, Combat, Kindness, Titles, or Luck.

summary.title: Endgame and the Final Push
bullets: Near victory: accelerate your Fame AND slow the leader
Attack leader's ship to destroy Cargo and force death-skip turn
Cannot buy final Fame Point — need concrete path (Exploration, Mission, Trade, Combat, Kindness, Title, Luck)
If leading: avoid combat, plan safe routes, have multiple Fame options ready

entries.definition: Starting-level ships. Smallest Holds, lowest Energy, basic Impulse Values. Tier 1 ships do not skip their next turn when destroyed, making them more forgiving for new players. Six Tier 1 ships are available during setup.

Mid-level ships with larger Holds, more Energy, and better stats. Cost 5,000 Credits to purchase during the Business Phase. Players keep all Ability Cards from previous ships. Tier 2 ships skip their next turn when destroyed.

Top-tier ships with the largest Holds, highest Energy, and strongest stats. Cost 8,000 Credits to purchase. Players accumulate 3 Ability Cards at this tier. Tier 3 ships skip their next turn when destroyed. Repair cost is 2,000 Credits.

A player board displaying the ship's Hold grid, Energy Meter, Armed/Disarmed spaces for 4 Markers, Impulse Space and Value, Bounty area, and the ship's name, Tier, and price. The back shows technical information, backstory, and the Victory Story.

A unique power card for each ship. To use, spend the required Energy, follow the card's instructions (often rolling a d20), then Exhaust (flip face down). Refreshed during Status Phase. Players keep all Ability Cards when upgrading ships.
A detailed ship model placed on a flight stand that occupies one hex space on the game board. No two ships can occupy the same space, but ships can move through occupied spaces.

The cargo grid on the Ship Mat representing the physical capacity of the ship. Outfits and Cargo Cubes are stored here. Damage Markers also fill Hold spaces. When Damage equals or exceeds Hold size, the ship is destroyed.
A track on the Ship Mat showing the ship's current Energy. Energy is spent to arm Markers, use Abilities, and scan Sectors. Recharges to maximum for free during Business Phase on any planet.

One of 4 Markers on your Ship Mat that can be spent to activate Outfits. Moved from an Armed space to a Use Space on an Outfit when used. Re-armed during Status Phase at a cost of 1 Energy each (free on planet).
A Marker that has been spent and not yet re-armed. Placed on a Disarmed space on the Ship Mat. Must be re-armed (1 Energy each) during the Status Phase before it can be used again.

A double-sided token placed on the Ship Mat. When face up (yellow side), Impulse is available. Flip face down to take a Move Action equal to the ship's Impulse Value at no Energy or Marker cost. Refreshed during Status Phase. Cannot be used for Ramming.

An Outfit type used for movement. Spend an Armed Marker, roll the Engine's die, and move up to the result minus Damage on the Outfit. Can also be used for Ramming (Attack at Range 1, but attacker takes full self-damage). Comes in 3 tiers with different dice: d6, d8, d12.

An Outfit type used for close-range attack at Range 1 with Line of Sight. Spend an Armed Marker, roll the die, and deal damage equal to the result. Each Damage on the Blaster reduces the roll by 1. Comes in 3 tiers.
An Outfit type used for long-range attack at Range 2-6 with Line of Sight. Cannot attack adjacent ships. Spend an Armed Marker, roll the die, and deal damage. Comes in 3 tiers.

An Outfit type used for defense. Unique because it can be activated outside your own turn when attacked. Roll the die to absorb incoming damage. Can also mitigate Asteroid damage and Nebula Energy loss. Markers spent on Shields can be re-armed immediately at 1 Energy each after an attack.

A space on an Outfit where an Armed Marker is placed when activating that Outfit. The number of Use Spaces limits how many times an Outfit can be used per turn. Damage Markers on Use Spaces block them.

Outfits are divided into sections by white dashed lines. Each section takes up one Hold space. Each section can hold one Damage Marker. The number of sections determines the Outfit's physical size and how many Hold spaces it occupies.
A Tier 1 Engine recommended for the Explorer strategy. Provides consistent movement for reaching unexplored edges quickly. Costs 2,000 Credits.
A Tier 1 Engine recommended for both the Trader and Pirate strategies. Balanced movement die suitable for general-purpose flying. Costs 1,000 Credits.
A Tier 1 Engine recommended for the Worker strategy. Provides reliable movement for mission running. Costs 1,000 Credits.
A Tier 1 Shield recommended for both the Trader and Worker strategies. Provides basic defense against attacks, Asteroid damage, and Nebula Energy loss. Costs 1,000 Credits.
A Tier 1 Blaster recommended for the Pirate strategy. Close-range attack Outfit with Range 1. Costs 1,000 Credits.
Solid-orange Cargo Cubes marked with an 'S'. Can be purchased at Kemplar II, Mined from Outpost 338, and sold at Neo Damascus. 15 Spice cubes in the game.
Translucent pink Cargo Cubes marked with an 'H'. Can be purchased at Azure, Harvested from Burning Horse, and sold at Kemplar II. 15 Holo cubes in the game.
Solid-green Cargo Cubes marked with a 'T'. Can be purchased at Doravin V, Mined from Red Gulch, and sold at Azure. 15 Terra cubes in the game.
Translucent teal Cargo Cubes marked with a 'P'. Can be purchased at Neo Damascus, Harvested from Lower Stratus, and sold at Lunari. 15 Plasma cubes in the game.
Solid-purple Cargo Cubes marked with a 'C'. Can be purchased at Lunari, Salvaged from Pelmont and Kreller IV, and sold at Doravin V. 15 Cyber cubes in the game.

A Lawful Mission type. Pick up passengers at a Mission Point, then deliver them to the specified Delivery Mission Point. Payment is based on the shortest flyable distance in Sectors between pick-up and delivery locations. No d20 roll required for the objective.

A Lawful Mission type. Fly to a Mission Point and conduct research with a d20 roll. Payment is determined by the quality of the research roll. Previously completed Research Missions can be spent to re-roll.

A Lawful Mission type. Destroy any Outlaw ship, then collect remains with a d20 roll. Payment depends on how intact the remains are. Deliver remains to the specified Mission Point. Combines combat with delivery.

An Outlaw Mission type. Pick up contraband at a Mission Point, then sneak past planetary detection at the Delivery planet with a d20 roll. Payment is based on the shortest flyable distance between pick-up and drop-off. Previously completed Smuggler Missions can be spent to re-roll.

An Outlaw Mission type. Fly adjacent to the target ship and attempt to steal goods with a d20 roll. Payment depends on the amount of goods acquired. Deliver goods to the specified Mission Point. Previously completed Thief Missions can be spent to re-roll.

An Outlaw Mission type. Destroy a specific target ship determined by seating order as stated on the Mission Card. Payment is based on the target ship's Tier. Deliver remains to the specified Mission Point. No d20 objective roll.
A space on a Sector Tile marked with a '#' symbol where players can Draw Missions (Minor Action) or Complete/Deliver Missions. Drawing requires no Active Mission; draw 3 cards, keep 0 or 1.

A player's currently accepted Mission Card, placed face down in their play area. A player may have at most 1 Active Mission at a time. Discarded if the player's ship is destroyed. Can be voluntarily aborted via Jettison Cargo Minor Action.

A mechanic that lets you discard a previously completed Mission of the same type to re-roll a d20 on a new Mission of that type. Only applies to Missions that require d20 rolls. Rewards specializing in one Mission type.
Represented by dashed white lines. No special rules for movement or Line of Sight. Most interior spaces within Sector Tiles are Normal Space.
Solid red lines representing the edges of Stars. Blocks Line of Sight. Any ship that crosses a Star Border is immediately destroyed. Stars are always fatal and cannot be shielded against.
Jagged solid orange lines. Blocks Line of Sight. Roll d20 to enter: 1-10 = take Damage equal to the roll (Shields can mitigate); 11-20 = safe passage. Exit without consequence.
Solid pink lines. Does not block Line of Sight. Roll d20 to enter: 1-10 = lose Energy equal to the roll (Shields can mitigate); 11-20 = no effect. Exit without consequence.
Solid yellow lines. Blocks Line of Sight. Roll d20 to enter: 1-3 = instant destruction (unblockable); 4-20 = safe passage. Exit without consequence.

Solid glowing lines (red, green, or blue). Blocks Line of Sight. Roll d20 to cross: 1-10 = blocked and take Damage equal to roll; 11-17 = pass through but gain 1,000cr Bounty; 18-20 = clean passage. Crossing is illegal.

Dashed glowing lines (lighter than Shields). Blocks Line of Sight. The legal way to enter/exit a planet. Special rules based on planet type: Lawful Entrances block Outlaw ships, Outlaw Entrances add 1,000cr Bounty to Innocent ships.

Solid green lines. Does not block Line of Sight. Can be moved through as Normal Space. Special movement: a ship on a Gate may spend 1 movement to teleport to any space on any other Gate. The Tigris Gate is malfunctioning and instead teleports to a random Spawn Point via d20 roll.

A planet type marked with a blue Planetary Shield and Entrance. Outlaw ships (those with Bounty) cannot enter through the Planetary Entrance but may attempt to sneak through the Shield. Seven planets exist in the base game.

A planet type marked with a red Planetary Shield and Entrance. Innocent ships (those without Bounty) that enter through the Planetary Entrance receive 1,000 Credits of Bounty for associating with Outlaws.
A planet type marked with a green Planetary Shield and Entrance. No special rules for entry beyond the standard Planetary Shield/Entrance mechanics. Any ship can enter freely through the Entrance.
A planet in the base game. Buy Space sells Spice Cargo Cubes. One of the recommended starting Sector Tiles for first games.
A planet in the base game. Sell Space buys Spice; Buy Space sells Plasma. One of the recommended starting Sector Tiles for first games.
A Sector in the base game that contains a Harvest Space for Holo Cargo Cubes. One of the recommended starting Sector Tiles for first games.
A planet in the base game. Sell Space buys Plasma; Buy Space sells Cyber.
A planet in the base game. Sell Space buys Terra; Buy Space sells Holo.
A planet in the base game. Sell Space buys Cyber; Buy Space sells Terra.
A planet in the base game. Buys Cargo Cubes of any color at its Sell Space. Home planet of the Scoundrel NPC.
An asteroid that functions as a planet. Sells Cargo Cubes of any color at its Buy Space. Has the same rules as a planet for Business Phase purposes.

The first phase of a player's turn. Players take Actions: Move, Attack, draw/complete Missions, buy/sell/mine/salvage/harvest Cargo Cubes, Explore (Scan or Blind Jump), use Abilities, Rescue stranded players, and Jettison Cargo. Continue until you cannot or choose not to take further Actions.

The second phase, only available if you end your Action Phase on a planet. Recharge Energy for free, repair Damage (1,000cr for Tier 1/2, 2,000cr for Tier 3), buy/sell Outfits, rearrange Hold, buy a new ship, or buy Fame (5,000cr for 1 Fame, cannot buy final point).

The final phase of every turn. Four steps in order: 1. Claim Fame (move Marker on Track). 2. Draw Titles (if first to reach a purple space). 3. Refresh Ability Cards and Impulse Token. 4. Arm Markers (1 Energy each, free on planet).

An Action that can be taken during a Move Action without ending the movement. Minor Actions include: Blind Jump, Collect Exploration Tokens, Collect Cargo Cubes, Draw Missions, and Jettison Cargo. Non-Minor Actions like Scan Sector end the Move immediately.

The victory currency of Xia. Tracked on the Fame Point Track. Earned through 9 methods: Exploration, Missions, Trade, Combat, Wealth, Ships, Kindness, Titles, and Luck. First player to reach the Victory Marker wins.
A track placed in the play area where each player has a Marker starting at 0 (Unknown). Purple spaces are Title spaces. The Victory Marker is placed at the agreed-upon winning total.

Placed on the Fame Point Track at the number of Fame Points needed to win. Suggested values: 5 FP (~15 min/player), 10 FP (~30 min/player), 20 FP (~45 min/player). The first player whose Marker reaches this point wins.

A small face-down token placed on Exploration Spaces when new Sector Tiles are revealed. When a player lands on the space, flip it to reveal the reward: 1 Fame Point, 1,000 Credits, or nothing (X). Once claimed, the token is not replaced. 21 tokens in the game.

An exploration Action (not Minor). Costs 1 Energy. Draw a Sector Tile from the deck, match the symbol, and place it adjacent to the player's current tile. Ends any current Move Action. Safer than Blind Jump because you see the tile first.

A Minor exploration Action. Costs 1 movement point. Declare an adjacent unexplored space, draw a Sector Tile, place it, and you must move into the declared space. Riskier than Scan (could land on a Star) but does not end your Move Action.

A marked space on a Sector Tile where ships begin the game and Respawn after death. When Respawning, roll d20 and place on the unoccupied Spawn Point closest to the number rolled. On a natural 20, choose any Spawn Point.

Credits placed on the Bounty area of a Ship Mat, indicating the ship is an Outlaw. Gained by: crossing Planetary Shields (11-17 roll), Outlaw Missions, or destroying Innocent ships. Claimed by whoever destroys the Outlaw. Removed on death or buying a new ship.
A ship with no Bounty Credits on its Ship Mat. Innocent ships can enter Lawful planet Entrances freely. Entering an Outlaw planet Entrance as Innocent adds 1,000cr Bounty.

A ship with Bounty Credits on its Ship Mat. Outlaw ships cannot enter Lawful planet Entrances and must sneak through Planetary Shields instead. The Outlaw Token can be placed on the flight stand to mark Outlaw status on the board.

A state where a player has 0 Energy and 0 Armed Markers. Stranded players can only move using Impulse and take Minor Actions. Unstranded by gaining Energy or Armed Markers (e.g., from being Rescued or reaching a planet).

An Action to help a Stranded player. The rescuer must be adjacent and have at least 3 Energy. Transfer 2 Energy to the Stranded player. The rescuer earns 1 Fame Point for Kindness. The rescued player may immediately arm Markers with the received Energy.

Using an Engine Outfit as an Attack Action at Range 1 with Line of Sight. The attacker rolls the Engine die for damage but also takes the full roll as self-damage (unblockable). If both ships are destroyed, the attacker receives no Fame or Bounty. Impulse cannot be used for Ramming.

A Minor Action to discard your Active Mission Card or drop Cargo Cubes from your Hold onto the board space your ship occupies. Jettisoned Cubes can be collected by other players. You cannot collect Cubes you jettisoned on the same turn.

A straight line from the center of the attacker's space to the center of the target's space. Blocked by Asteroids, Debris Fields, Stars, Planetary Shields, Planetary Entrances, and unexplored spaces. Required for Blaster, Missile, and some Ability attacks.
The distance in spaces between two ships on the game board. Blasters have Range 1 (adjacent only). Missiles have Range 2-6. Some Abilities specify their own Range. Range is counted in hexes.

A token placed in a ship's Hold to represent damage. Each Marker takes 1 Hold space. Placed on Outfit Sections, it reduces die rolls by 1 and blocks Use Spaces. Placed on a space with a Cargo Cube, the Cube is Jettisoned. Hold full of Damage = ship destroyed. 80 Damage Markers in the game.

The currency of Xia, represented by metal coins: 30 silver 1,000-Credit coins and 10 blue 5,000-Credit coins. Earned through Exploration Tokens, Missions, selling Cargo Cubes, collecting Bounties, free trade with other players, and Ship Abilities. Spent on repairs, Outfits, ships, Cargo Cubes, and Fame.
An Action taken on a planet's Buy Space. Pay 1,000 Credits for 2 Cargo Cubes of the type specified on the Buy Space. Must have empty Hold space. The Smugglers' Den sells Cargo Cubes of any color.

An Action taken on a planet's Sell Space. Sell matching Cubes for 1,000 Credits each. If you sell all Cargo in your Hold and sold at least 2 Cubes, earn 1 Fame Point for Trade. Planet Loath buys Cargo Cubes of any color.

An Action on a Mining Space in an Asteroid Field. Roll d20: 1-10 = take Damage equal to roll (Shields can mitigate); 11-20 = receive 1 Cargo Cube of the type shown on the Mining Space. Can attempt multiple times per turn on the same space.

An Action on a Salvage Space in a Debris Field. Roll d20: 1-3 = instant destruction (unblockable); 4-20 = receive 1 Cargo Cube of the type shown on the Salvage Space. Can attempt multiple times per turn. Highest risk resource gathering.

An Action on a Harvest Space in a Nebula. Roll d20: 1-10 = lose Energy equal to roll (Shields can mitigate); 11-20 = receive 1 Cargo Cube of the type shown on the Harvest Space. Can attempt multiple times per turn.

A special achievement card drawn when a player first reaches a purple Title space on the Fame Point Track. Read aloud and placed face up for all players. Any player can claim by completing the stated objective during their Status Phase. Awards 1-3 Fame Points and a permanent Skill. 18 Title Cards in the game.
A permanent bonus granted by claiming a Title Card. Skills enhance Actions or provide new powers. Immediately available once the Title is claimed. Read each Title Card for specific Skill details.
A Lawful NPC that targets nearby Outlaw ships, moves toward them, and attacks. Patrols between Lawful planets when no Outlaws are nearby. Can never have a Bounty. Retaliates when attacked.

An Outlaw NPC that hunts Innocent ships. After attacking, flees toward its home planet of Loath. Always has a Bounty, so it cannot enter Lawful planets. Retaliates when attacked but never targets the Enforcer.

A Neutral NPC that travels along a trade route between planets. Gains 1,000 Credits each time it arrives at a planet's Sell Space. Becomes a tempting target for pirates as it accumulates wealth. Does not actually buy/sell Cargo Cubes.

The four dice used in Xia. The d6, d8, and d12 correspond to Outfit tiers (Tier 1, 2, and 3 respectively). The d20 is used for Ability Cards, border crossings, Missions, Mining, Salvaging, Harvesting, Respawning, and Luck rolls.
A reference card given to each player during setup that summarizes the nine ways to earn Fame Points. 5 How To Win Cards are included in the game.
A visual aid placed on a Miniature's flight stand to help differentiate ships on the board. Players should use the color matching their set of Markers. 5 Color Tokens in the game.
A visual aid slid over a Miniature's flight stand to denote that a ship has a Bounty and is therefore an Outlaw. 7 Outlaw Tokens in the game.

A large hexagonal tile that makes up the game board. Each tile has 19 hex spaces, unique borders, and edge symbols for alignment. Contains features like Spawn Points, Mission Points, Exploration Spaces, Buy/Sell Spaces, and special spaces. 21 tiles in the game.

A measurement in Sector Tiles used for some Mission payments. Count the fewest tiles you must enter to fly from the pick-up to delivery location. Do not count unexplored space or Star Sectors. Gate special movement is never used in this calculation.
Rolling a 20 on the d20 before any modifiers. Earns 1 Fame Point for Luck, maximum once per turn. Applies to any d20 roll during a player's turn. One of the nine ways to earn Fame Points.

Two ships are adjacent when they occupy directly neighboring hex spaces. Planetary Shields and Entrances break adjacency — to interact with a ship on a planet, you must also be on that planet. Different from Line of Sight; ships can be adjacent without Line of Sight.

When a ship crosses two borders with a single movement point, both border effects are applied in the order they are crossed. For example, exiting through a Planetary Shield and entering a Nebula requires rolling for both borders in sequence.

A player may take an Action on an adjacent space occupied by another ship by spending 2 extra movement points. The player does not move but can act as though on the occupied space. Must still roll for any borders passed through.
